perturbation.original_text: For a period of 5 years from the date of this Agreement, the receiving party shall not publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party accept to those of its own employment having valid need to know the information in the course of employment and such disclosure shall be on terms not less restrictive than those herein contained.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of 1 year from the date of this Agreement, the receiving party shall not publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party accept to those of its own employment having valid need to know the information in the course of employment and such disclosure shall be on terms not less restrictive than those herein contained.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 1 year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, as the reasonable duration of protection depends on the specific trade secret and industry standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Reducing the contractual protection period to 1 year may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient, especially if the trade secret retains its value for a longer duration, potentially undermining the protections intended by CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from 5 years to 1 year. The Law Explanation indicates that under California law, trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. The Perturbation Explanation states that a 1-year period may be insufficient, potentially undermining the protections intended by CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), providing a foundation for the concern that reducing the protection period could be deemed unreasonable if the trade secret's value extends beyond one year.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the disclosing party of such Proprietary/Confidential Information orally discloses the information to the receiving party, the disclosing party agrees to promptly notify the receiving party of the confidentiality of such oral disclosure and reduces to writing such Proprietary/Confidential Information and submit the same to the receiving party within 15 days of such oral disclosure, upon which the receiving party shall not be bound by the confidentiality obligations as herein provided as regards the said Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed orally.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the disclosing party of such Proprietary/Confidential Information orally discloses the information to the receiving party, the disclosing party agrees to promptly notify the receiving party of the confidentiality of such oral disclosure and reduces to writing such Proprietary/Confidential Information and submit the same to the receiving party within 60 days of such oral disclosure, upon which the receiving party shall not be bound by the confidentiality obligations as herein provided as regards the said Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed orally.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the period for reducing oral disclosures to writing to 60 days may render trade secret protection ineffective under California law, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires "efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy." A 60-day delay in documenting oral disclosures could be deemed unreasonable, thereby jeopardizing trade secret status and contradicting the reasonable efforts standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the period for reducing oral disclosures to writing from 15 to 60 days. This creates a contradiction because California law, as suggested by the LLM's law explanation, requires "efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy." While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Misappropriation" in the context of trade secrets and emphasizes the duty to maintain secrecy. A 60-day delay could be deemed unreasonable, thereby jeopardizing trade secret status and contradicting the reasonable efforts standard to protect trade secrets, making the new obligation unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall govern all matters referred to herein until terminated by either party upon thirty days written notice to the other or in accordance with this Agreement. Upon termination, all information and materials shall be returned to the respective parties. Notwithstanding the termination, each party shall continue to fulfill its obligations hereunder for a period of five (5) years thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall govern all matters referred to herein until terminated by either party upon five days written notice to the other or in accordance with this Agreement. Upon termination, all information and materials shall be returned to the respective parties. Notwithstanding the termination, each party shall continue to fulfill its obligations hereunder for a period of one (1) year thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 5 days and the post-termination confidentiality period to 1 year creates potential conflict with trade secret protection under California law, potentially undermining reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. A short 5-day notice period for termination could be deemed insufficient for a disclosing party to take appropriate steps to protect its trade secrets before the agreement ends. Similarly, a 1-year post-termination obligation could be inadequate if the trade secret's value extends beyond that period.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 5 days and the post-termination obligation from 5 years to 1 year. The Law Explanation states that the CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy and a short notice period might be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, and that a one-year post-termination obligation may be inadequate. The Perturbation Explanation indicates a potential conflict with trade secret protection under California law, undermining reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code (CIV) 3426, part of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Although Snippet 1 is primarily a citation, the Law and Perturbation Explanations point to the need for 'reasonable efforts' which the reduced timeframes could undermine, thus creating a legal contradiction as per the Inconsistencies definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall not make any assignment of the Agreement or any interest therein without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may make any assignment of the Agreement or any interest therein without the need to obtain any prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior written consent for assignment could violate established principles of contract law in California, particularly if the contract involves personal services or a relationship of trust and confidence.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 1457 does not explicitly prohibit assignment, it implies that obligations involving personal skills or qualifications cannot be freely transferred. Removing the consent requirement could lead to an unenforceable assignment if it materially alters the original agreement or prejudices the non-assigning party's rights.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. TRANSFER OF OBLIGATIONS [1457 - 1471] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1457. The burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise, except as provided by Section 1466. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text requires prior written consent for assignment, while the changed text removes this requirement. This directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1457, which states that "the burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise."
